poster_title: INSIDE: LLMS’ INTERNAL STATES RETAIN THE POWER OF HALLUCINATION DETECTION
authors: Chao Chen, Kai Liu, Ze Chen, Yi Gu, Yue Wu, Mingyuan Tao, Zhihang Fu, Jieping Ye
paper_info: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

content: Knowledge hallucination in LLMs poses security and reliability risks.
Previous methods lose semantic information through logit-level or language-level evaluations.
A novel approach is needed to detect hallucinations effectively.
Token-level uncertainty measures are insufficient for sequence-level uncertainty.
Post-hoc semantic measurements on decoded sentences are less effective.
Overconfident hallucinations are challenging to detect.
INSIDE framework uses LLMs' internal states for hallucination detection.
EigenScore metric measures semantic consistency in the sentence embedding space.
Feature clipping reduces overconfident generations.
Evaluated on popular LLMs and QA benchmarks.
Compared different sentence embeddings and feature clipping strategies.
Achieved state-of-the-art performance.
State-of-the-art hallucination detection performance.
EigenScore captures semantic consistency and represents differential entropy.
Feature clipping aids in detecting self-consistent hallucinations.
Internal states of LLMs are powerful for hallucination detection.
EigenScore and feature clipping improve LLM reliability.
The framework is effective across various LLMs and benchmarks.
INSIDE framework leverages LLMs' internal states for hallucination detection.
The methods improve the reliability of LLMs in practical applications.
Future work will explore the framework's applicability to other NLP tasks.
